--- SOURCE 2: 3 Important Crisis Management Trends for 2023 ---
URL: https://padillaco.com/post/3-important-crisis-management-trends-for-2023

SUMMARY:
3. Important. Crisis. Management. Trends. for. 2023 · 1. Organizational Values · 2. Direct Stakeholder Contact · 3. Core Response Teams.

FULL CONTENT:
# 3 Important Crisis Management Trends for 2023

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/plugins/Basic/assets/placeholder.60f9b1840c.svg)

Media Relations

January 25, 2023

Chris Werle

As we enter a new year, itâs important to re-evaluate our preparedness for potential critical issues/incidents. Here are three crisis management trends that will most likely continue to grow in importance as executive leadership teams seek to protect their institutions from serious reputational harm.â¯Â

**1. Organizational ValuesÂ**

Employees are demanding more from their organizations when it comes to corporate social responsibility, and this affects its actions and communications in a crisis. For this reason, organizational values and principles will play a more prominent role in crisis plans and serve as more of a guide in crisis decision-making. Already, weâre seeing executive leaders take the values of an organization as a key element in discussions around ransomware payments, employee layoffs and severance packages.â¯Â

**2. Direct Stakeholder Contact**â¯Â

With an ever-growing list of tools available to reach stakeholder audiences directly, crisis management planning is prioritizing these channels to reach key constituencies with unfiltered and targeted messages. Timely and thorough information shared directly to employees, partners, suppliers, regulators, legislators, customers, etc. mitigates undue concern and creates an immediate opportunity for important feedback that will assist in management of a current crisis.â¯Â

**3. Core Response Teams**â¯Â

While a large crisis response team spanning multiple functions and departments looks impressive in a plan, in practice, the effort to assemble such a substantial and potentially decentralized team can waste precious time in the early stages of a swiftly developing and evolving crisis. Strategic crisis plans are moving toward engaging smaller core response teams. This group is tasked with making immediate early decisions that can mean the difference between a well-contained and managed incident and a major crisis causing long-term reputational damage. These decision-makers and strategists can gather information quickly and remain the operational center of the crisis while bringing in expertise or resources from the larger organization as needed.â¯Â

As you embark on this new year consider including these three crisis management trends as important areas of focus in 2023. If you need help with your crisis management strategy or want to learn more about our Corporate Advisory Group, [connect with us](https://padillaco.com/contact-us/). â¯â¯Â

*For our thoughts on communication and brand strategy, industry trends and more, subscribe to Padilla InsightsÂ*[*here*](https://buzzbinpadillaco.com/about-thebuzzbin/#subscribe)*.*

Curated thoughts from curious thinkers.Â

SUMMARY:

![Wag The Dog Newsletter logo](https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/publication/logo/a6166327-a663-42a7-88d8-774e7c713658/thumb_Wag_The_Dog_Newsletter.png)

# Crisis Trends: the ICM Annual Crisis Report Analysed

## In this edition I am looking at the Institute for Crisis Management's (ICM) 2023 Annual Crisis Report.

![Author](https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/user/profile_picture/4a8f2a48-abaf-4ee9-a25f-880870c223f1/thumb_secta.ai_004.jpg)

[Philippe Borremans](https://www.wagthedog.io/authors/4a8f2a48-abaf-4ee9-a25f-880870c223f1)  
 August 01, 2024 • Estimated Reading Time: 10 minutes

![](https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/4a406940-ef15-4cc6-963a-8dfaed7cebce/horationelson23_Horizontal_illustration_in_the_style_of_The_New_424556da-84af-4514-961f-e836ac9be8e2.png?t=1722503429)

**In partnership with**

![](https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/ad_network/advertiser/logo/6c54e14e-a401-4ca3-93ca-ff9fdf208524/GS_Logo_Full__2_.png)

Dear reader,

Sorry… this edition of the Wag The Dog Newsletter is a day late! 😅

In this edition I am looking at the Institute for Crisis Management's (ICM) [2023 Annual Crisis Report](https://crisisconsultant.com/icm-annual-crisis-report/?utm_source=www.wagthedog.io&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=crisis-trends-the-icm-annual-crisis-report-analysed).

[Deborah Hilleman](https://www.linkedin.com/in/hilewoman/?utm_source=www.wagthedog.io&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=crisis-trends-the-icm-annual-crisis-report-analysed), the CEO of the ICM, does a fantastic job every year of analysing the general trends in crisis communication.

The report provides an in-depth look at the evolving crisis landscape, highlighting the key trends and changes that have characterised the past year.

From the increasing impact of natural disasters to the rise of cyber threats and legal action, these insights are vital for organisations looking to stay ahead.

Let’s check out the findings. Enjoy!

## Table of Contents

[Key findings from the ICM Annual Crisis Report 202 …](#key-findings-from-the-icm-annual-cr)

[Overall decline in crisis news:](#overall-decline-in-crisis-news)

[Trends shaping the crisis landscape](#trends-shaping-the-crisis-landscape)

[Climate change and natural disasters:](#climate-change-and-natural-disaster)

[Increase in litigation:](#increase-in-litigation)

[Cybersecurity Concerns:](#cybersecurity-concerns)

[Workplace issues:](#workplace-issues)

[Staying Prepared: Recommendations for businesses](#staying-prepared-recommendations-fo)

[Methodology](#methodology)

[Recap](#recap)

[References and further reading.](#references-and-further-reading)

[Sponsor](#sponsor)

[What I am reading/testing/checking out:](#what-i-am-readingtestingchecking-ou)

### **Key findings from the ICM Annual Crisis Report 2023**

The Institute for Crisis Management (ICM) has published its Annual Crisis Report for 2023, and provides a comprehensive analysis of the global crisis landscape.

This year’s **report**[1](#b-38c58d99-885a-4cf9-b1e5-f6b0a875672b "1 Annual Crisis Report | Institute for Crisis Management. (2024, July 31). ICM Crisis Consultants. https://crisisconsultant.com/icm-annual-crisis-report/") highlights significant changes in various crisis categories, reflecting broader societal and environmental changes. Here is an overview of the key findings:

##### **Overall decline in crisis news**:

The total number of crisis news stories decreased by 9% in 2022 and totalled 1,977,722 stories in 2023.

##### **Balanced crisis types**:

The ratio between sudden and smouldering crises is almost 50:50 again and corresponds to the 2020 level.

##### **Declining categories**:

Five categories saw a decline: accidents, consumer activism, executive dismissals, sexual harassment and whistleblowers.

##### **Increase in disasters**:

Disasters accounted for 26.10% of crisis reports, up from 20.81% in 2022, primarily due to natural disasters.

##### **Impact of natural disasters**:

In 2023, 399 natural disasters caused 86,473 deaths and affected over 93 million people worldwide.

Economic losses from these events totalled more than USD 250 billion, exceeding the 20-year average.

##### **Record weather events in the USA**:

The U.S. experienced 28 separate weather and climate disasters with billions in losses, more than ever before.

##### **Legal and cyber trends**:

Class action lawsuit reporting increased from 7.70% in 2022 to 11.37.

Cybercrime reports more than doubled from 3.21% to 7.24%.

Reports of discrimination also doubled from 3.44% to 7.19%.

##### **Environmental and corporate issues**:

Reports of environmental damage increased from 0.07 in 2022 to 0.47%.

Hostile takeovers rose from 0.42% to 3.71%.

Reports of white-collar crime increased from 1.8% to 10.8

Reports of workplace violence rose to 9.39%.

### **Trends shaping the crisis landscape**

The report highlights a dynamic and evolving crisis landscape for 2023. Here are some key trends:

#### **Climate change and natural disasters**:

The increase in disaster declarations and economic losses from natural disasters highlights the increasing impact of climate change[3](#b-d0e1e2a2-33d0-484a-9e4e-fcd2f1402450 "3 sigma 1/2024: Natural catastrophes in 2023. (2024, March 26). Https://Www.swissre.com/Institute/Research/Sigma-Research/Sigma-2024-01.Html; Swiss Re Institute. https://www.swissre.com/institute/research/sigma-research/sigma-2024-01.html") . Organisations need to prioritise resilience and preparedness for extreme weather events.

#### **Increase in litigation**:

The significant rise in class action litigation demonstrates the increasing legal scrutiny and demand for corporate accountability. This trend reflects increasing public and legal activism against corporate misconduct.

#### **Cybersecurity Concerns**:

With reports of cybercrime more than doubling[2](#b-f946704a-0ae8-418e-b942-a3a725c050c2 "2 Rising Cyber Threats Pose Serious Concerns for Financial Stability. (2024, April 9). IMF. https://www.imf.org/en/Blogs/Articles/2024/04/09/rising-cyber-threats-pose-serious-concerns-for-financial-stability") , digital security remains a critical area of concern. Companies need to invest in robust cyber defences and remain vigilant to new threats.

#### **Workplace issues**:

The rise in workplace violence and white-collar crime points to underlying societal and economic stressors. Organisations need to create a safe and ethical working environment.

### **Staying Prepared: Recommendations for businesses**

The ICM report stresses the importance of being prepared for crises. Here are the key recommendations:

**Involve senior management**: Ensure that board members are actively involved in crisis management and decision-making processes.

**Invest in planning and training**: Regularly update crisis communication plans and conduct comprehensive training for employees at all levels.

**Evaluate vulnerabilities**: Conduct thorough risk assessments to identify and mitigate potential vulnerabilities.

**Stay current**: Keep contingency plans up to date with new threats and best practises.

### **Methodology**

The [Institute for Crisis Management](https://crisisconsultant.com/?utm_source=www.wagthedog.io&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=crisis-trends-the-icm-annual-crisis-report-analysed) (ICM) conducts an annual analysis of global economic crises, categorising 1,977,722 crisis news stories from 2023 into 16 categories such as natural disasters, cybercrime and workplace violence.

ICM uses quantitative and qualitative analyses and compares the current data with that of previous years to identify trends and shifts in the crisis landscape.

Supplementary data from sources such as the [Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters](https://www.emdat.be/?utm_source=www.wagthedog.io&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=crisis-trends-the-icm-annual-crisis-report-analysed) (located in my home country of 🇧🇪) and the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission are included for a comprehensive overview.

### **Recap**

The ICM Annual Crisis Report for 2023 offers valuable insights into the changing crisis landscape. From natural disasters to cyber threats, organisations must remain vigilant and proactive in their crisis management efforts.

By understanding these trends and implementing robust preparedness strategies, organisations can manage the complexity of today’s crisis environment and protect their operations and reputation.

## References and further reading.

--- SOURCE 4: Crisis management trends into 2023 - December2022 ---
URL: https://aprioupdate.h5mag.com/december2022/crisis_management_trends_2023

SUMMARY:
Cyber-attacks will remain the biggest threat facing enterprises in 2023. You are assailed virtually daily with scary cyber data. Cybercrimes will cost the

FULL CONTENT:
# Crisis management trends into 2023

It’s that time of the year when many marketing and public relations consultants make bold, speculative predictions about trends they think will get some momentum in 2023. Here’s some crystal ball gazing from Aprio Credence on a few crisis and reputation management trends that we predict (and hope) will get some traction in the new year:

You are assailed virtually daily with scary cyber data. Cybercrimes will cost the global economy $10.5 trillion annually by 2025. The latest ransomware is reported to be close to 60 times more destructive than the ransomware of 2015. Aprio Credence’s annual research confirms that cybercrime is perceived to be the most prevalent risk among our biggest listed enterprises. How prepared is your company to mitigate the reputation risks of a cyber-attack in 2023?

Aprio Credence’s annual research shows that ESG risk was the fast growing enterprise risk category over the past three years. Global research, and our own experience of advising on more than 400 crises, confirms that most crises have their roots “inside the building” – they are management and employee misconduct issues. ESG might be the popular new label for these sorts of risks, but Aprio Credence believes most of our crisis management advisory work in 2023 will continue to be focused on the “social” and “governance” elements of ESG such as incidents of alleged racism, sexual harassment, fraud, unethical conduct, poor corporate governance and the like – as it has been for more than 15 years.

We are seeing growing corporate awareness of the value of investing in a series of single-issue crisis management “playbooks” for their top reputational risks – from cyber-attacks, regulator interventions, workplace fatalities or kidnapping for ransom, through to executive misconduct, engineering failures, or sponsorship controversies. Our playbook “order book” for 2023 is already filling up, with a range of different types of potential crises for which clients want to develop detailed response plans.

It has long been the complaint of reputation management specialists that they don’t have a seat at the crisis leadership table. Aprio Credence has seen a slow but steady shift in the way Boards and C-suites view and treat the discipline of professional reputation management – and we anticipate that with the rise of ESG as a top tier risk dimension reputational capital will increasingly be viewed as a highly valuable strategic asset in 2023.

Our final trend may sound flippant, but the reality is that in 2023 we still expect to see many big enterprises continue with a posture of “We don’t need to invest in crisis preparedness and resilience, because it will never happen to us.” At Aprio Credence, we would love to see more organisations take reputation management seriously, build greater crisis preparedness, skills and structures, have world-class playbooks and protocols in place, and stress test them regularly through simulations and other exercise. But the reality is that such crisis-ready organisations will continue to be in the minority in 2023.

NEXT ARTICLE:

[**Four digital trends corporate communicators should look out for in 2023**](/december2022/digital_trends)

SUMMARY:

FULL CONTENT:
Crisis news story statistics continued their post-pandemic shuffle with some surprises revealed in the 2023 numbers. The Institute for Crisis Management® (ICM) tallied 1,977,722 crisis news stories in 2023, a decrease of about nine percent over 2022 in a retreat towards pre-pandemic story numbers. COVID-19 stories continued to make some headlines, but not nearly to the extent of the previous three years. The volume of sudden versus smoldering crisis stories migrated back to-ward the 2020 levels with a nearly 50/50 split. Just five of the 16 categories tracked had a lower result than the previous year: casualty accidents, consumerism/activism, executive dismissals, sexual harassment and whistleblowers. CATASTROPHES This category accounted for 26.10% of the total, an unforeseen increase from 2022’s 20.81%. ICM had expected this number to continue its down-ward trend as COVID retreated, but the number of natural disasters that occurred across the globe drove the category upward. In rare instances, we observe an inverse relationship with year-over year numbers and percentages. In the case of this cate-gory, there were nearly 69 thousand more stories tallied in 2023, but the percent of the total dropped by ten points over the prior year. Accord-ing to a report by the Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters (CRED), there were 399 natural disasters in 2023 causing 86,473 fatalities and impacting more than 93 million people. Economic losses were estimated at more than $250 billion USD, higher than the averages report-ed over the previous 20 years. Asia accounted for 163 of the reported natural disasters, followed by 98 in the Americas, 61 in Europe and 60 in Africa. Storms and floods accounted for 139 and 164 disasters, respectively. In terms of the human toll, 73.4% of the deaths occurred in Asia, 24.6% in Africa and 20.6% in the Americas. More than 62 thousand deaths were due to earthquake, with 55 thousand of them attributed to a February earthquake that hit Turkey and Syria. Among the deadliest natural disasters last year: More than 100 people died when wildfire swept through Hawaii’s Maui Island and town of Lahaina. In May, Rwanda had heavy rain and flooding that killed more than 120 people and destroyed 5,000 homes. Cyclone Mocha killed at least 145 in Myan-mar. Floods in the Democratic Republic of the Congo claimed another 438 lives. In November, more than 150 lives were lost when a 5.6 magni-tude earthquake struck in Nepal. In Afghanistan, nearly 1,500 people lost their lives when a 6.3 magnitude quake shook the western part of the country. In the United States, 28 separate weather and climate events cost at least $1 billion each, the highest number of billion-dollar disasters ever recorded in the U.S. CASUALTY ACCIDENTS Casualty accidents again accounted for less than one percent of the total at 0.28%. While higher than the pandemic years, the percent is consider- ICM ANNUAL CRISIS REPORT 2023 GLOBAL NEWS COVERAGE OF BUSINESS CRISES IN 2023 Issued July 2024 © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management®. All Rights Reserved. ® 2023 CRISIS CATEGORY RESULTS © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 2 CATEGORY 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 Catastrophes 2.26% 37.15% 36.61% 20.81% 26.10% Casualty Accidents 0.68% 0.06% 0.11% 0.13% 0.28% Class Action Lawsuits 1.19% 3.83% 4.73% 7.70% 11.37% Consumer Activism 1.82% 7.44% 7.81% 8.76% 0.94% Cyber Crime 13.28% 2.93% 1.58% 3.21% 7.24% Defects and Recalls 2.59% 1.03% 1.10% 0.83% 1.67% Discrimination 11.92% 9.73% 3.52% 3.44% 7.19% Environmental Damage 2.26% 0.40% 0.07% 0.02% 0.47% Executive Dismissal 2.19% 2.47% 5.55% 5.80% 2.49% Hostile Takeovers 1.17% 0.33% 0.29% 0.42% 3.71% Labor Disputes 10.62% 1.30% 1.90% 3.19% 4.65% Mismanagement 19.56% 3.90% 3.95% 7.18% 8.53% Sexual Harassment 16.43% 3.77% 2.98% 6.35% 3.09% Whistleblower 8.13% 22.08% 25.94% 28.96% 2.08% White Collar Crime 4.32% 2.06% 1.94% 1.80% 10.80% Workplace Violence 1.59% 1.52% 1.91% 1.39% 9.39% Other: Casualty Accidents 0.28%; Consumerism/ Activism 0.94%; Defects and Recalls 1.67%; Environmental Damage 0.47%; FIVE-YEAR TRENDS BUSINESS CRISIS DEFINED Any issue, problem or disruption which triggers negative stakeholder reactions that can impact the organization’s reputation, business and financial strength TYPES OF BUSINESS CRISES Sudden Perceptual Smoldering Bizarre bly lower than pre-pandemic levels. According to the U.S. Federal Railroad Administration, there were 2,192 train collisions with 248 fatalities and 765 injuries. These statistics represent slightly more incidents but fewer casualties. It was an exceptional year for commercial avia-tion. According to online news site FlightGlobal, there were six fatal accidents in 2023 with 115 deaths. None of the crashes were jetliners. Both figures are half the prior year’s totals. In January, a Yeti Airlines plane went down, killing 68 passen-gers and four crew members. In February at LAX Airport, an American Airlines plane collided with a shuttle bus, injuring five. In February, five people died in the crash of a plane headed from Little Rock to Columbus, Ohio. The five worked for an environ-mental response consulting firm and were headed to the site of a metal factory explosion. In January, Boeing pleaded not guilty to allegations of mislead-ing federal authorities following two deadly 737-MAX crashes, in 2018 and 2019. An air medical transport plane went down in February enroute from Reno, Nev., to Salt Lake City, killing all five aboard. On the roadways, a bus carrying high school stu-dents to a band camp tumbled down an embank-ment in New York, killing two adults and seriously injuring several others. The company, Regency Transportation Ltd., has been cited for more than 25 violations since 2021. In Pennsylvania, a power-ful explosion at the R.M. Palmer chocolate factory killed seven, injured ten and damaged several build-ings. In October in Illinois, a tanker truck carrying anhydrous ammonia collided with other vehicles, causing multiple fatalities. CLASS ACTIONS Class action stories skyrocketed by nearly fifty percent to 11.37% of stories tracked. According to Cornerstone Research, there were 83 settlements in 2023, 22 fewer than in 2022. Among these, 34% settled for more than $25 million, the highest percentage since 2012. Securities class action suits recovered nearly $8 billion for plaintiffs globally. Among the high-profile cases resolved in 2023 were two against mega bank Wells Fargo for an aggre-gate of $1.2 billion and a $450 million settlement with food giant Kraft Heinz. Other settlements in-cluded Exelon, $173 million; Allianz Global Inves-tors, $145 million; McKesson, $141 million; and Car-dinal Health, $109 million. In fiscal 2023, the U.S. Equal Employment Oppor-tunity Commission (EEOC) received 81 thousand complaints, filed 143 merits lawsuits and resolved 98 suits, obtaining more than $22 million for victims of employment discrimination. Ninety-two of the new suits were for alleged Title VII violations. Title VII prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex and national origin. Allegations included retaliation and discrimi-nation based on gender, disability, rage, age and religion. Another 49 suits alleged violations of the Americans with Disabilities Act. CONSUMERISM/ ACTIVISM This category tanked in 2023, dropping to 0.94% from 8.76% in 2022. In one of the most notable events, beer maker Anheuser Busch’s Bud Lite beer lost its luster and its place atop the market follow-ing an ill-conceived promotion for March Madness (the annual NCAA basketball tournament) with transgender activist Dylan Mulvaney. Conservative backlash helped drive a 13.5% drop in revenue at its U.S. division. Social movements grew despite less media cov-erage. In the U.S., activism activity around causes like gun safety, climate change, abortion access and 3 © 2016 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 3 transgender healthcare continued to make inroads. In January, a group of young activists gathered in Las Vegas demanding that industry leaders priori-tize their safety. Students in Florida organized a mass walkout of schools in protest to some of Governor DeSantis’ conservative legislative moves. Students also played a key role in a Nashville, Tenn., demonstration following a school shooting. On the other side of the political spectrum there were increasing numbers of boycotts against companies that have supported the LGBTQ+ community, including Target, Disney, Starbucks and Barstool Sports. CYBER CRIME This category more than doubled, to 7.24% of stories tracked, from 3.21% the previous year. This figure is still well below the pre-pandemic activity we saw in 2018 and 2019. According to cybersecuri-ty firm SonicWall®, attacks increased across the board. Malware attacks increased 11% year-over-year, encrypted threats were up 117% and cryptojacking was up 659%. Threat hackers have continued to use tried-and-true methods. And also have turned to generative AI technology to refine their attacks and carry out convincing business email compromise (BEC) attacks. The Identity Theft Resource Center (ITRC) report-ed 3,122 reported data breaches in 2023 affecting nearly 350 million victims. Healthcare and financial services continued to lead with 809 and 744 com-promises, respectively. According to a study by IBM and the Ponemon Institute, the average total cost of a breach in 2023 was $4.45 million, an increase of 2.3%. Since 2020 the average cost of an incident has increased more than 15%. Interestingly, just 51 % of companies surveyed planned to increase secu-rity spending following a breach. And, according to the study, only one in three attacks were identified internally. Among the biggest cyber-attacks in 2023, tech provider Johnson Controls received a $51 million ransom demand from hackers who claimed access to 27 terabytes of data. Ten million customers of MGM Resorts had their PII—personally identifiable information—exposed, costing the company $100 million. U.S. discount retailer Dollar Tree was hit with a supply chain attack that compromised the PII of 2 million people. In Australia, port operations were shut down when critical infrastructure was hit, bringing the movement of 30 thousand shipping containers grinding to a halt. Chinese hackers cov-ertly accessed email accounts at more than two dozen U.S. government sites. An attack on cellular phone provider T-Mobile in January affected 37 million customers. DEFECTS AND RECALLS Still lower than its pre-pandemic levels but dou-ble the previous year, this category accounted for 1.67% of stories tracked in 2023. A news release from the Public Interest Network reported more than 300 food recalls in 2023, the highest level in three years. There were 506 U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) recalls, a five-year high. U.S. Department of Agriculture recalls also increased year over year. Lead-tainted applesauce sickened 500 children across the U.S. and tainted cantaloupe killed six and sickened more than 400. Government regulators reported 65 recalls of meat and poultry products totaling 4 million pounds of product. In 4 © 2016 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 4 December, the Quaker Oats Company recalled 25 granola bar varieties and eight granola cereal varie-ties due to a salmonella risk. In the U.K., the Food Standards Agency warned consumers about dangerously high caffeine levels in SCI-Mx Nutrition Ultra Muscle, prompting a recall. According to a news release from Sedgwick Claims Management Services, U.S. product recalls increased 11% over 2022, a seven-year high. In its State of the Nation Recall Index Report, Sedgwick identified 3,301 events affecting 759 million units across five industries. There were 975 recall events in the medical device industry, representing more events but fewer total units impacted. Pharmaceu-tical recalls reached a nearly 20-year high, increas-ing more than 42% and driven by increased sterility -related events. While the number of automotive recalls decreased in 2023, the number of recalled units increased to 38.4 million. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) reported 56 electric vehicle recalls totaling 566,584 vehicles, representing an increase in events but a significant decrease in affected vehicles. The majority of the EV recalls affected Tesla and Lucid vehicles. More than 6 million vehicles were recalled due to issues with Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). Other notable automotive recalls included 10,600 Polaris ATVs recalled for a fire hazard; 3.3 million Kia and Hyundai vehicles for fire-related issues; 319,000 Empower air fryers recalled for burn hazards; 37,000 Traeger Flatrock flat top grills recalled for fire hazard; 50,000 Rhino Tree Stands recalled due to a fall hazard; and 4.8 million BlendJet portable blenders due to fire and laceration hazards. DISCRIMINATION The year saw this category more than double year-over-year in a trend back toward pre-pandemic levels at 7.19% of stories tracked. Age discrimination is alive and well in the U.S., accord-ing to a study released by the Society of Human Resource Management (SHRM) in May. According to the report, 32% of HR professionals surveyed said that applicant age played a role in hiring decisions. The study found that common age stere-otypes still prevail with perceptions that older workers are not as competent with technology, resistant to change and stubborn or grumpy. The EEOC reported that in fiscal 2023, the agency secured settlements of more than $665 million for discrimination victims, a nearly 30% in-crease over the prior year. The EEOC, focusing its efforts on systemic discrimination, also reported that it had received 81,055 new complaints during its most recent fiscal year. In one of the most widely covered incidents, a Black student in Texas was told he would be sent to a disciplinary program over his hairstyle, prompting his parents to file a formal complaint with the Texas Education Agency, calling the school policy discriminatory. Various versions of hair-related policies have been enacted at local levels in several states on both sides of the issue. The U.S. House of Represent-atives attempted to pass fed-eral legisla-tion with the CROWN (Create a Respectful and Open World for Natural Hair) Act, but it failed to pass in the Senate. ENVIRONMENTAL DAMAGE This category was practically nonexistent in 2022 at 0.07%, but the number of stories increased exponentially to account for a 0.47% slice of the crisis news ‘pie’ in 2023. The biggest U.S. environ-mental incident of the year happened in northeast Ohio in February when a Norfolk Southern Railroad 5 © 2016 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 5 train derailed in the town of East Palestine. Thou-sands were evacuated from their homes and many report ongoing health issues due to the toxic mess that resulted from the event. The train comprised 38 railcars, including 11 tank cars carrying hazardous materials. The lingering impacts of the incident, in-cluding a company decision to release and burn the toxic vinyl chloride in the tank cars, are expected to add up to more than $1 billion. Reaction and commentary about the rail freight industry focused on working conditions and employ-ee safety concerns, includ-ing the lack of modern brake safety regulations and implementation of PSR, or precision sched-uled railroading. Critics claim that companies have failed to invest in mainte-nance, choosing instead to return excess profits to stockholders. According to a story in The Guardian, chemical accidents happen about eve-ry two days in the U.S. The spills come from a variety of events, including derailments, truck crashes, pipe-line leaks and industrial spills. In September, 300 people were evacuated and nine hospitalized after a spill at a California recycling facility. A large pipeline rupture in Kansas in December spilled nearly 600 thousand gallons of diluted bitumen crude oil, and with a cleanup cost of nearly $500 million. Multiple agencies of the U.S. federal government are involved with hazmat incidents, making it hard to determine a precise number of incidents. Other notable events included a leak and fire at a Marathon Petroleum refinery in Louisiana; a hydro-gen fluoride/chlorine leak at a Honeywell blowing agent factory in Louisiana; a storage tank filled with naphtha ignited after being struck by lightning, again in Louisiana; a fire at a plastics facility in Brunswick Ga. that led to a city-wide emergency, evacuations and shelter-in-place advisories for those living more than one-half mile from the plant. In March, at a Trinseo Altuglas chemical plan in Bristol, Pa. several thousand gallons of butyl acetate, ethyl acetate and methyl methacrylate spilled into Otter Creek, a tribu-tary of the Delaware River. EXECUTIVE EXITS This category has bounced around over the past several years, from a low 1.00% in 2018 and a high of 5.8% in 2022 to just 2.49% last year. According to a report from recruiting firm Challenger, Gray & Christ-mas, there were 1,914 CEO exits in 2023, an increase of 55% over the prior year and surpassing a record set in 2019. The firm also reports that CEOs are leav-ing their posts younger, with an average age of 56 in 2023, down from 63 in 2017. Companies did not report rea-sons for the CEO departures in 33% of exits. Twenty-one percent retired and 22% moved into advisory roles. The retail sector experienced several notable executive exits during the year. Bankrupt retailer Bed, Bath & Beyond lost two CEOS in as many years, includ-ing the CEO of Overstock who oversaw the acquisition of BB&B out of bankruptcy. Dollar General also had a one-two punch when the compa-ny replaced CEO Todd Vasos with Jeff Owen and then brought Vasos back after 11 months. Executive search firm Heidrick & Struggles reported that retail executive turnover was up 17% in 2023. Other nota-ble retail exits included GameStop, Walgreen’s, Dol-lar Tree, Rite Aid, Joann and Wolverine Worldwide. HOSTILE TAKEOVERS This category increased exponentially, to 3.71% in 2023 compared to just 0.42% the previous year. In Bruce, ACT, Australia, Calvary Public Hospital failed to fend off the Australian government from a hostile takeover of the facility. The facility, formerly owned by the Catholic Little Company of Mary Health Care, is now owned and managed by the ACT government. In April, Choice Hotels launched a hostile takeover of rival Wyndham after the company rejected an unso-licited $8 billion buyout offer. The transaction was still uncertain at year’s end. South Africa telecom company Telkom rejected a bid launched by its former CEO Sipho Maseko and an investor 5 © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 6 6 © 2016 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 7 consortium including UAE-based rival Axian Telecom. Public Storage made an unsolicited offer to acquire rival Life Storage. Instead, Life Storage merged with Extra Space Storage, making the combined company the largest U.S. storage opera-tor. Public Storage later acquired Simply Self Storage from the Blackstone REIT. A merger between rival budget airlines Jet Blue and Spirit, originally announced in 2022, languished in numerous regulatory issues. LABOR ISSUES This category crept upwards with 4.65% of sto-ries tracked, still well below a recent pre-pandemic high. According to the Economic Policy Institute, major strike activity in the U.S. grew by 280% for the year. Data released by Bloomberg Law noted 345 strikes and two employer lockouts. Work actions had the most active year since 2000. Among the largest work stoppages was an 82-day strike by 160 thousand SAG-AFTRA members against Hollywood studios. The Writers Guild launched a simultaneous strike against the same Hollywood producers. In a precedent-setting move, 53,700 United Auto Workers members launched a 43-day strike against Ford, General Motors, Stellantis and Mack Trucks. A coalition of 75,600 union members staged a three-day walkout against healthcare giant Kaiser Permanente. In the Philippines, a series of strikes by transpor-tation providers lingered for several months, protesting the government’s plan to phase out older and WWII-era vehicles for more eco-friendly models. Additional work actions included teacher strikes in Lebanon, Los Angeles, South Korea, New Zealand and Romania; school bus drivers in Alaska; German public transport workers; workers at Tem-ple and Rutgers Universities; taxi drivers in Cape Town, South Africa; city workers in Prince Albert, Saskatchewan; and casino workers in Detroit. MISMANAGEMENT This typically high category continued to defy expectations with just 8.53% of stories tracked in 2023, well below its 2017 high of 27%. Some of the change is attributed to higher results in other cate-gories, including white-collar crime. Silicon Valley Bank gave us one of the most noteworthy cases of mismanagement. In the largest bank failure since 2008, SVB customers, dominated by tech startups, drove a run on the bank forcing regulators to step in. The collapse was quickly followed by the collapses of Signature Bank and First Republic Bank, forcing them into receivership and ultimate acquisition by larger banks. Credit Suisse also failed miserably as a result of risk practices and gaps in oversight. Regulators stepped in and brokered an acquisition by UBS. Another dramatic collapse was that of WeWork, once a Silicon Valley mainstay. A failed IPO and later takeover, followed by impact of the pandemic were too much for the company to stay afloat. Retailer Bed Bath & Beyond made a series of errors that led to its downfall, including a stock buyback twice as large as the company’s debt and a too-slow move to online retailing. SEXUAL HARASSMENT Sexual harassment stories came in at 3.09% for 2023, less than half the percentage compared to the previous year. This category has bounced up and down since the #metoo movement of 2018 drove it upwards and the pandemic drove it down. This is the second category that in 2023, had an inverse relationship of numbers and percentages year-over-year. In 2023, there were 75 thousand fewer stories, but the category percentage increased over 2022. In New Jersey, the Newark Public Schools declined to investigate dozens of © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 8 sexual harassment complaints and failed to take appropriate action, resulting in a resolution agree-ment with the U.S. Department of Education for nine Title IX violations. The EEOC filed suit on behalf of an Arkansas woman who, after refusing to resign was fired in retaliation for reporting sexual harassment charges against a physician. In another EEOC action, Mueller Co. LLC, an Alabama maker and distributor of gas and water products, was ordered to pay $150 thousand and sign a three-year consent decree to settle allegations of sexual harassment and attempted assault. In Maryland, the U.S. Justice Department reached a settlement with Baltimore County regarding allegations of a hostile work environment at the Baltimore County Fire Depart-ment. Restaurant oper-ator Chipotle settled an EEOC suit alleging sexual harassment against young female employees for $400 thousand to “avoid the distraction of continued litigation.” In an interesting Ari-zona case, the EEOC sued pet store Bark If You’re Dirty under Title VII, alleging it failed to prevent and remedy sexual harassment and illegal retaliation against female employees who complained. In No-vember, a bill was introduced in the U.S. House of Representatives aimed at clarifying the role and responsibilities of a Title IX coordinator in a K-12 school and elevating the voices of students of color, LGBTQ+ students, and students with disabilities who are more often victims. Activision Blizzard Settled a sexual harassment case, originally filed in 2021, for $54 million. The lawsuit alleged the com-pany fostered a culture that encouraged misogyny and sexual harassment. In Australia, new laws require employers to take proactive measures to prevent workplace sexual harassment. A survey on sexual harassment at work had revealed that one third of people had experi-enced sexual harassment on the job. The UK Parlia-ment also expanded protections for workers that require employers to take ‘reasonable steps’ to prevent sexual harassment. WHISTLEBLOWERS This category dropped well below its pre-pandemic levels to just 2.08% of stories tracked last year. The category peaked in 2020-2022 with thou-sands of complaints against employers for failing to provide a safe work environment during the COVID pandemic. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) reported it received 18,000 tips and granted $600 million in awards in the fiscal year ended September 30. Awards dropped sharply despite the increase in reports. A single informant received the largest payout, $279 million, in agency history. The agency says it needs additional resources to follow up on increasing complaints. The agency has award-ed $1.6 billion to whistleblowers since its program began in 2011. Among SEC enforcement actions last year, the Agency charged that D.E. Shaw & Company road-blocked whistleblowing by forcing employees to sign agreements prohibiting disclosure of confiden-tial information to third parties without excepting the SEC. Dallas-based commercial real estate com-pany CBRE, Inc. forced employees to sign a release saying that they had not filed a complaint against CBRE with any federal agency. Nebraska-based Monolith Resources LLC was charged with requiring certain departing employees to explicitly waive their right to monetary whistleblower awards. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), like the SEC, also reported a record number of tips in fiscal 2023. WHITE COLLAR CRIME Mismanagement stories shrank last year, and part of the reason may be due to an exponential increase in this category, to 10.8% of stories tracked, compared to 1.8% the previous year. White-collar crimes typically include things like fraud, money laundering and public corruption. Among some of the most memorable cases in 2023, in a huge healthcare fraud scheme, the U.S. Justice Department announced a national enforce-ment action including charges against 78 people and more than $2.5 billion in alleged fraud. Allegations included people facing state and feder-al charges in 16 states. In one of the biggest white-collar stories in 2023, FTX founder Sam Bankman Fried was charged with wire fraud, securities fraud and money laundering, bilking investors out of more than $26 million. And in a family saga that includ-ed fraud and murder, South Carolina lawyer Alex Murdaugh, already imprisoned for the murder of his wife and son, pleaded guilty to 22 counts of theft from personal injury clients. Charges includ-ed money laundering, forgery, criminal conspiracy, wire fraud and tax evasion. In Florida, an attorney dubbing himself ‘the lottery lawyer’ was charged with defrauding clients who had won huge lottery payouts. The lawyer was sentenced to 13 years in federal prison for his actions. Two California residents pleaded guilty in July to conspiring to and committing wire fraud in a $115 million scheme defrauding inves-tors. In Idaho, a federal grand jury indicted two executives of competing companies for conspiring to rig bids for forest firefighting services in viola-tion of the Sherman Act. United Memorial Medical Center in Houston, Tex., agreed to pay $2 million to resolve allega-tions of violations of the False Claims Act. An Ohio man confessed to his role in conspiracies to commit health care fraud and to pay and receive illegal kickbacks under the Federal Anti-Kickback statute. In Georgia, a chiropractor admitted to a $14.9 million healthcare fraud and kickback scheme related to durable medical equipment and cancer testing. WORKPLACE VIOLENCE An unfortunate and significant increase in this category emerged with 9.39% of stories tracked. Many of the cases reported arose from worker-on-worker violence. In New Jersey, a former PSE&G employee killed a coworker in the parking lot, later turning the gun on himself. At a Family Dollar store in St. Louis, a woman shot a coworker in the face following an argument. In Half Moon Bay, Calif., a mushroom farm employee shot four coworkers and another three former coworkers at a nearby farm. In Louisiana, a recently fired man returned to the workplace and murdered two ex-coworkers. Similar worker-on-worker shootings occurred in Denver, Indianapolis, Prairie View, Tex., Evansville, Ind., Louisville, Ky., Arlington, Tex., Detroit, Alamogordo, N.M., Philadelphia, Richmond, Va., Charlotte, N.C., Conshohocken, Pa. and San Diego. There were more than 600 mass shootings in the U.S. in 2023, an average of about two per day. When it comes to mass shootings, it is notable that of 36 countries studied by the Rockefeller Institute of Government, the U.S. makes up 33% of the population and 76% of public mass shooting incidents. © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 9 © 2024 Institute for Crisis Management. All Rights Reserved. 10 TAKEAWAYS Among the most notable results from the 2023 study were the two inverse relationships between raw numbers and percent of the total crisis pie. Likewise, downward trends in the percent of stories in five categories don’t necessarily inform a trend. The results have been so topsy-turvy over the past four years that ICM hesitates to make any predictions of what 2024 will bring. enriched_points.expanded_content: Organizations are shifting towards single-issue crisis management and prioritizing online reputation management, reflecting a more focused approach to crisis preparedness.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of technology in crisis management
enriched_points.expanded_content: Technologies like AI and real-time communication tools enhance crisis response efficiency, allowing for quicker decision-making and better stakeholder engagement during incidents.
Proactive crisis management strategies rooted in organizational values help mitigate risks and ensure alignment with stakeholder expectations, ultimately protecting reputational capital.
Stakeholders increasingly demand transparency and direct communication during crises, necessitating organizations to adapt their messaging and outreach for effective engagement.
